data.title: Nvidia: Why Its Lead Over Competitors May Be As Short As One Year
data.description:
Performance assessment

Nvidia (NVDA) has slightly beaten the S&P 500 since my last update on the stock:

Thesis

Right now, my bullish view on Nvidia's revenue prospects and lower valuations is balanced by my concerns about competitive pressures and slowing momentum in the stock's price action:

  1. Leading revenue indicators are very robust
  2. Competitive pressures are creeping in and Nvidia may be losing some bargaining power
  3. Valuations are more attractive
  4. The bullish momentum is slowing down

Leading revenue indicators are very robust

It is encouraging to see an increasing revenue trend in Foxconn (OTCPK:FXCOF) (OTCPK:HNHAF) (OTCPK:HNHPF) as this company supplies more than 50% of Nvidia's AI hardware:

Nvidia is also making a very clear statement that the longer-term revenue growth outlook is very robust, as evidenced by an acceleration in the company's remaining performance obligations [RPO] YoY growth, particularly for net new beyond NTM RPOs, which has seen a QoQ jump of $360 million:

Remaining performance obligations are a leading indicator of revenue.

The CFO also highlighted that the company has visibility to achieve $500 billion in revenues from Blackwell and the next-generation Rubin chips by the end of CY26:

We currently have visibility to $0.5 trillion in Blackwell and Rubin revenue from the start of this year through the end of calendar year 2026.

- CFO Colette Kress in the Q3 FY26 earnings call

For context, the company is currently tracking at $187 billion in TTM revenues:

Competitive pressures are creeping in and Nvidia may be losing some bargaining power

Rising receivables concentration from Nvidia's major cloud service provider customers may be indicative of an incrementally weaker bargaining power position for Nvidia:

the share of receivables attributable to major customers rose from an average of 23.8% in FY2020–2024 to 65% in Q3 FY26.

- TF International Securities Analyst Ming-Chi Kuo

On a related point, Ming-Chi Kuo also expressed that rising receivable days for Nvidia is not a cause for concern from the perspective of financial irregularities or fraud. I agree with his view here. In other words, my issue with rising receivable days is due to economic implications, not financial integrity concerns.

There are also tangible signs of key customers taking steps to reduce reliance on Nvidia. For example, Microsoft (MSFT) is making moves to replace Nvidia and Advanced Micro Devices (AMD)'s chips with its own Maia AI accelerators. Amazon (AMZN) is also investing in its Trainium and Inferentia chips, which yields 30-40% better costs vs Nvidia's GPUs. Even OpenAI (OPENAI) is looking to partner with Broadcom (AVGO) to develop its own AI chips. And finally, perhaps the biggest threat right now is that Google's (GOOGL) (GOOG) Tensor Processing Unit [TPU] AI chips provide a more cost-effective (lower total cost of ownership [TCO]) alternative to NVIDIA's Blackwell chips for a particular computing performance level:

TPUv7 closes most of the distance on raw throughput and memory versus GB200, then leans on TCO. From Google’s side the all in cost per chip at rack scale is meaningfully lower than a GB200 or GB300 system because they are not paying Nvidia’s full system margin. SemiAnalysis models that for a big, sophisticated user like Anthropic, TPUs can deliver roughly 30 to 50% lower cost per effective PFLOP than GB300 if you invest in the compiler work and get solid MFU. Even if you only get around half the utilization of a GB300, you can still end up even or ahead on cost.

- Wall St Engine, SemiAnalysis

PFLOP stands for Peta (10^15) floating point operations per second. This is a measure of chip computing performance.

MFU refers to model FLOP utilization, which is a measure of how much of the theoretical peak computing performance is actually utilized. What the passage is saying is that if an enterprise invests in optimizing how its AI models map onto Google's TPU hardware (aka compiler work), it can achieve a higher MFU.

GB300 refers to Nvidia's Blackwell configuration combining both GPUs and CPUs.

Importantly, Google's challenge and lower TCO advantage are not merely theoretical, as Meta (META) is actively exploring a multi-billion dollar deal to use Google's AI chips.

Interestingly, Nvidia has issued a direct statement in response, claiming that its chips remain a generation ahead of the overall industry:

But I am not so assured by this because arguably the length of a generation lead is hardly 1 year, as I had noted in my last article how Nvidia itself is releasing a new generation of chips every year. So overall, I expect Nvidia to feel the pressure from these competitive threats in future quarters. This may materialize via gross margin pressures:

It could also materialize via a worse net working capital days profile due to higher receivables:

Valuations are more attractive

Relative to its semiconductor sector comps, NVDA's 1-yr fwd PE of 25.9x is trading at a 10.0% discount to the sector median of 28.8x. This represents a meaningful discount, as NVDA has typically traded at a 9.6% premium vs peers.

It is also encouraging to see the valuation multiple remain flattish to slightly lower, whereas the earnings expectations index is rising:

This is an indicator that there is no overhype in the stock right now.

The bullish momentum is slowing down

If this is your first time reading a Hunting Alpha article using Technical Analysis, you may want to read this post, which explains how and why I read the charts the way I do. All my charts reflect total shareholder return as they are adjusted for dividends/distributions.

Relative Read of NVDA vs SPX500

On the relative monthly charts of NVDA vs SPX500, I see signs of the bullish momentum slowing down. This is because the incremental length of the impulse moves up between consolidation levels is getting shorter. Slowing momentum generally increases the chances of a deeper trend correction or reversal. Hence, I am getting increasingly more cautious about NVDA stock.

Takeaway & Positioning

If we look solely at Nvidia, the numbers and commentary paint a very rosy picture, as the revenue growth prospects are still extremely healthy. But if we look at what Nvidia's customers are doing, we can get clues that they are not only gaining in bargaining power at the negotiation table (as evidenced by longer receivable days for Nvidia), but also looking to reduce reliance on Nvidia's chips. And in this regard, the spotlight is on Google and its TPUs, which industry experts say provide a compelling lower TCO alternative to NVDA's Blackwell chips for similar performance by some measures.

The following chart shows how Google's TPUs have almost on-par computing performance vs. Nvidia's Blackwell chips but fall short on memory capacity:

I expect this to make Google's TPUs a serious contender, particularly for inference workloads, as that does not require as high memory capacity as training workloads.

Overall, I think this is a big sign of caution for the bulls on NVDA stock, even if Nvidia is a generation ahead of competitors. This is because, given the rapid pace of new product development, a generation implies a very short timeframe of 1 year.

On the valuations side, I like NVDA stock as it is trading at a rare relative discount vs. peers. However, this plus point is arguably offset by slowing momentum in NVDA stock.

Rating: 'Market Perform/Hold'

How to interpret Hunting Alpha's ratings:

Strong Outperform/Strong Buy: Expect the company to outperform the S&P 500 on a total shareholder return basis, with higher than usual confidence. I also have a net long position in the security in my personal portfolio.

Outperform/Buy: Expect the company to outperform the S&P 500 on a total shareholder return basis

Market Perform/Hold: Expect the company to perform in-line with the S&P 500 on a total shareholder return basis

Underperform/Sell: Expect the company to underperform the S&P 500 on a total shareholder return basis

Strong Underperform/Strong Sell: Expect the company to underperform the S&P 500 on a total shareholder return basis, with higher than usual confidence

The typical time horizon for my views is multiple quarters to more than a year. It is not set in stone. However, I will share updates on my changes in stance in a pinned comment to this article and may also publish a new article discussing the reasons for the change in view.

data.title: Synopsys: The Hidden Champion Behind The AI Arms Race
data.description:
Introduction

It happened once again. Nvidia (NVDA) announced on Monday that it has taken a $2bn stake in chip design software maker Synopsys (SNPS), making it one of its largest shareholders. The news led both firms' shares higher, as Synopsys climbed roughly 9% on the week, with Nvidia shares up by about 3%. This represents the latest of several similar announcements in recent months, all of which involved some form of investments or partnerships between Nvidia and various companies in the AI value chain. These moves are no coincidence, but rather strategic investments that will help the world's most valuable company strengthen its position in this rapidly evolving race of artificial intelligence. But what exactly does Synopsys do that makes it so important to Nvidia's success? Let's take a deep dive into the businesses that Synopsys operates in and analyze what makes it so crucial in the modern age of AI.

Thesis

Synopsys is the irreplaceable foundation on which modern high-performance chips are being built on. With its leading position in Electronic Design Automation software, all major chip designers are among its customers, including Nvidia, AMD (AMD), Google (GOOG) or Apple (AAPL).

As the age of AI is only getting started, the race to build the most powerful and efficient silicon is well underway. To make this possible, chip design teams have built their workflows around Synopsys' offerings, making it nearly impossible to switch to competitors. The firm is cooperating tightly with fab operators like TSMC (TSM) to offer customers leading design capabilities that take advantage of the impressive production technologies available.

In addition to offering EDA software, Synopsys is a leading provider of highly advanced chip components, that the firm licenses out to its customers creating a high margin revenue stream that accelerates with rising chip production volumes.

Last but not least, by acquiring simulation software maker Ansys earlier this year, Synopsys is effectively creating an end-to-end solution that spans from designing the logical structure of the chip to the physical simulation of its functionality inside a broader system. This will help Synopsys create value for its customers along several major steps in the chip production value chain, justifying potential price increases in the future.

With shares still down roughly 8%, even after its recent spike, I believe that the opportunity for price appreciation is attractive.

Electronic Design Automation (EDA)

The Electronic Design Automation (EDA) segment is the core of Synopsys' business. It primarily focuses on software solutions that are helping engineers to design, verify and prepare chips for production. As of Q3 2025, the segment accounted for 68.6% of total revenues. In essence, EDA can be divided into three parts based on the underlying functions.

First, there is the Front-End-Design. This is where the blueprint of the chip is created, determining which functions it should have. You can compare it to a CAD software that helps with converting a first draft into a detailed plan for a building, but for chips consisting of billions of transistors.

Next, EDA includes Back-End-Design. Simply speaking, this is where the exact arrangement of transistors on the silicon wafer is being planned.

Last, the Emulation and Verification business makes sure that a chip design is fully functional and has no bugs before it gets send to the manufacturer.

Looking at its role in the overall semiconductor value chain, Synopsys takes a foundational position that all other industry participants rely on. As can be seen in the graphic below, while directly providing its software to chip designers like Nvidia, AMD or Apple, it works closely with foundries as well as Integrated Device Manufacturers (IDMs) to make sure that its solutions work hand in hand with manufacturing capabilities and vice versa.

Revenue generation in the EDA segment is extremely reliable and has high visibility due to its recurring nature. The customer agrees on multi-year subscriptions to get access to the various tools that Synopsys has to offer. These subscriptions are typically non-cancellable, which creates a high degree of forecastability for the firm based on the backlog it built. While there is still a small number of perpetual licenses out there, the trend towards a subscription offering has been developing for a long time.

The importance of Synopsys' offerings to the chip industry is immense. It is no exaggeration to say that, without the company, modern chip architecture and production wouldn't be possible. As chip designs are getting more and more complex, powerful software capable of keeping up with rapidly rising performance needs is crucial.

The market for EDA is concentrated, as only three companies are controlling nearly three quarters of the total market. Besides Synopsys, which currently holds an estimated global market share of 31%, the two other relevant competitors are Cadence Design Systems (CDNS) with roughly 30%, and Siemens EDA (OTCPK:SIEGY) which holds an estimated 13% share.

The moat that Synopsys has built in the EDA business is really strong. The primary reason for that is related to the high switching costs that customers that are using the solutions face. They have built entire workflows, scripts and intellectual property around Synopsys tools, which would make a switch to a different offering a multi-year project with massive risks. On top of that, the already mentioned tight integration with foundries like TSMC or Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF) creates a natural barrier for competitors since it ensures that Synopsys' software always enables designers to take advantage of the latest advancements in manufacturing capabilities. The company is widely viewed as the technological leader in the space, with decades of research and development efforts that are separating it from peers. This includes its AI suite of products that can automate large parts of the workflow, significantly reducing design time and energy consumption.

Design Intellectual Property (IP)

Within this segment, Synopsys offers its customers a wide range of chip components that it develops on its own and chip designers can implement in their designs. Since a chip not entirely consists of highly unique components, but also standard parts and blocks, customers can license them to avoid developing everything on their own. Synopsys owns one of the largest portfolios of intellectual property in the chip design industry. A large part of that portfolio is related to high-speed interfaces, that allow the chip to communicate with other systems. Examples are high-bandwidth memory interfaces, DDR and LPDDR memory controllers, or Ethernet and USB standards. Synopsys also offers processors for embedded systems in automotive and IoT applications, as well as security related IP.

But why are firms buying IP from Synopsys instead of developing it themselves?

Simply speaking, it is faster, saves costs and reduces risk. The development of high-speed standards often takes years to complete. Investing a lot of time and money into own solutions comes with the risk of falling behind against known players like Synopsis that already have expertise in the field. Being able to rely on fully functional, high performing components that allow designers to focus on their unique developments are a huge benefit in the race of offering the best and most capable chips on the market.

With its offerings, Synopsys is positioning itself as a global leader. It earns money in two ways in the segment. First, the firm receives a one-time license fee when a customer implements one of Synopsys' components into the chip design. However, monetization gets even better considering the company earns royalties for every chip that is manufactured containing Synopsys IP. Since these parts are essential to modern chips, the firm directly benefits from rising volumes in chip manufacturing for AI and HPC, as well as smartphones.

Multi-Physics & System Simulation

Traditionally, Synopsys has been a chip design software provider. In July of this year, the firm acquired Ansys for approximately $35bn. Ansys offers software for advanced multi-physics simulations and analysis. The deal is strategically important for Synopsys, as it improves its competitive position from an EDA focused provider to an end-to-end player in the chip design chain. Ansys' software enables engineers to simulate physical attributes of product designs, including mechanics, thermals, electromagnetic, fluid dynamics and much more. Why is that important and valuable in the broader chip design industry?

Chips are not isolated, but rather imbedded in larger systems. A server processor works inside a large rack, an AI accellerator needs appropriate cooling and so on. The interaction of the chip and its surroundings in a physical way is highly important to understand. Without simulations, designers would have to manufacture prototypes, that would likely lead to many costly revisions and long development times. Instead, Ansys' software allows for the early identification of weak points in the design, so engineers can adjust it accordingly.

The integration of EDA and simulation offerings creates a competitive advantage for Synopsys, which peers can't replicate. Switching costs for customers are increasing when an entire end-to-end workflow is tied to a single software provider. Synopsys says that the deal significantly increases its Total Addressable Market (TAM), now estimated at approximately $31bn. This not only results from higher pricing power and cross-selling opportunities, but also a broader customer base, including industries like automotive and aerospace that rely on advanced physical simulations.

Key Growth Drivers

Synopsys' growth is primarily tied to the growth in advanced chip manufacturing. However, since a significant part of their revenues comes from stable and recurring subscriptions, it is not a cyclical business in nature, as customer churn rates are incredibly low. US Hyperscaler CAPEX forecasts are still very high for the upcoming years, as can be seen in the chart below.

This leads me to believe that the appetite for more advancements in chip design is not going anywhere. Leading GPU designers like Nvidia and AMD will continue to invest billions of dollars in future product lines, like Rubin and Feynman over the coming years. As these products are getting continuously more complex, powerful and reliable EDA is irreplaceable.

The recent debate about the competitiveness of custom ASICS might be a hurdle for Nvidia and AMD to overcome, but Synopsys will thrive nevertheless, since more chip designing and manufacturing will act as a tailwind for its EDA and IP segments. Also, the fact that an increasing number of companies is actively developing and designing their own chips will help diversifying the customer base. As an example, Apple designs its own A- and M-series chips, Tesla (TSLA) is developing chips for its autonomous vehicles, SpaceX for satellite communication and so on. This stabilizes the growth outlook in case any of the customers would scale back on their spending.

Last but not least, as manufacturing nodes advance, the cost associated with the design of the chip also goes up, as pictured in the graphic below.

This acts as another direct tailwind that should help Synopsys continue to grow its topline at a fast rate. With leading-edge nodes below 7nm making up a sizable portion of today's chip production, it is clear to see the upside for the firm looking forward.

Valuation

To determine whether Synopsys is a good investment right now, we have to take a look at the relative valuation versus industry peers.

EV/EBITDA (FWD) EBITDA Margin (TTM)
Synopsys (SNPS) 32.28 20.38%
Cadence Design Systems (CDNS) 33.18 36.12%
Siemens (OTCPK:SIEGY) 14.97 16.45%
Arm Holdings (ARM) 61.16 23.84%
Rambus (RMBS) 27.37 42.50%
Dassault Systèmes (OTCPK:DASTY) 13.57 24.88%
Average 30.42 27.36%

Using an EV/EBITDA relative valuation method, I calculated the average multiple for the peer group of chip design software and IP providers, as well as simulation software firms, which is 30.42 at the time of this article.

Based on the numbers above, Synopsys seems to be slightly overvalued, considering its inferior profitability metrics. However, we have to take into account that the peer group, besides Cadence Design Systems, doesn't consist of pure-plays. This is especially important for the valuation, but also for the comparison of margins.

If we are just looking at Synopsys and Cadence, we can observe that the two companies trade at a comparable multiple. However, Cadence has higher margins, which stems from the fact that it focuses more on software, and has a much smaller IP business, which is more capital intensive. Going forward, the acquisition and integration of Ansys will likely be a drag on profitability metrics for a couple of quarters, but should eventually pay off through cross-selling opportunities, and the potential for price increases.

Risks to the Thesis

The risk that is currently most important to the firm is related to the acquisition of Ansys. Not only the price tag of the deal is very large, but the acquired company is a very complex one, which could create potential integration issues, making it difficult to realize synergies. If cross-selling opportunities are being delayed because of that, investors could react by discounting the value of the deal.

Also related to the Ansys acquisition are regulatory risks. After deal was announced, several regulatory authorities raised their concerns over the planned acquisition, but ultimately granted their approval. However, this shows that regulatory scrutiny is a risk that investors should be aware of, since Synopsys operates in a highly concentrated industry.

In my view, the last important risk to consider is tied to the growth in semiconductor R&D spending. A larger downturn could have a material impact on earnings, even though this risk is more relevant long-term, since subscription revenues are stable and highly visible.

Conclusion

Nvidia's $2bn stake in the company brought Synopsys back into the spotlight. Rightfully so in my opinion, since the firm holds one of the most important positions in the chip industry. After a less-than-satisfactory performance of its shares this year, I could see momentum coming back to help the stock catch up to its fundamentals.

While the valuation isn't cheap, on a relative basis, it seems plausible considering the business is performing well and has several catalysts going for it over the coming years.

As a result, I initiate coverage on Synopsys with a Buy rating.

data.title: Miniso Group Holding: Broad-Based Strengths Justify A Buy Rating
data.description:
Investment action

I had a buy rating for Miniso Group Holding (MNSO) previously, as I was bullish on the accelerating growth trends across segments and regions. The enhanced capital return policy was also a bullish factor. Nothing in Q3 has changed my view. With broad-based momentum, solid execution, and operating leverage kicking in, the setup into FY26 looks stronger. In my view, the risk-reward is still positive.

Latest Earnings Review

MNSO delivered a strong Q3 that dismisses the idea that consumer spending is weak. Revenue grew 28.2% y/y to RMB5.8 billion, beating the high end of guidance, and the strength was broad-based. The Miniso brand in China grew 19.3% y/y, overseas revenue grew ~28% y/y, and TOP TOY reported solid growth at 111% y/y. All of these were done with heavy investments, but margins still held up. Gross margin came in at 44.7% vs. 44.9% last year, a small drop that I don’t think really matters given the overall top-line growth. And besides, the slight drop was really due to MNSO shifts toward more local sourcing in markets like the US. On an absolute basis, adj. EBIT still grew 14.8% y/y to >RMB1 billion for the first time, while adj. net profit grew 11.7% y/y to RMB767 million.

China SSSG Acceleration

It appears that I was right about China previously. Same-store sales [SSS] accelerated, with domestic SSS up a high single digit in Q3, which is a lot better than China’s broader retail growth of just 3.4% (mid-single-digit relative share gain). The good news is that momentum has continued into Q4, with management noting that SSS growth has accelerated to low teens in October. This sets up MNSO really well for Q4; that overall growth should accelerate as well.

Context matters here. This acceleration also matters because it shows the strategy is working. MNSO’s push toward interest consumption (i.e., IP-driven items that are tied to brands like Harry Potter and Chiikawa) is helping MNSO to capture demand. In some way, you can think of this as a “product edge,” since MNSO is the only one that sells this item, consumers that want it have to come to MNSO. We could argue that the next batch of IP-driven items do not resonate with consumers’ preferences, but based on the data today (including the accelerated SSS growth going into Q4), it sure seems like MNSO has found the recipe to offer the right product.

I also like to touch on one of the long-term bear cases for MNSO China. The narrative is that MSNO has hit a ceiling in China’s top cities (i.e., no more white spaces to open new stores). The Q3 results dismiss this since the pace of growth accelerated. MNSO added 102 net new stores, and that alone offset all the net closures from 1H25. The operators of these stores (the franchisee) do not open stores for new reasons. There must be indicators that the ROI makes sense, and the fact that openings picked up while SSS accelerated suggests the domestic market still has room to grow.

US Market

This quarter’s results also finally gave hard evidence that MNSO’s shift from chasing scale in its overseas markets to chasing profitable scale is working. The US market is the focus here. Overall, the overseas market growth was great at ~28% y/y, but within it, the US market was a key driver. Revenue in the US grew >65% y/y, driven by low-double-digit SSS growth. But the bigger story here is the margin line. Management explicitly called out a step-up in EBIT margins for directly operated markets, mainly the US and Canada. And remember, this happened even with the gross-margin drag from local procurement that I mentioned above.

I have a few takeaways here that make the equity story even better. Firstly, the fact that overseas markets are growing much faster than China helps with the diversification story. If this trend continues, the narrative of a weaker China macro will weigh less on the stock. Secondly, the sheer magnitude of growth suggests that MNSO’s brand is resonating with US shoppers, and very importantly, the growth accelerated even when the revenue base was larger. This tells me that there is still a lot of room for MNSO to scale (usually, one would expect growth to decelerate given the larger revenue base due to the law of large numbers). Finally, I think we can also expect margin to inflect higher in the coming quarters as operating leverage kicks in. My view is that as store utilization goes up, the fixed costs (like logistics and rent) are spread across a larger revenue base, and that should lead to higher margins.

TOP TOY

I am also surprised that TOP TOY has done so well, now becoming a more tangible growth driver. As said, this segment grew revenue 111% y/y, which is a very impressive headline number, but the more important detail is that SSS growth still came in at mid-single-digits. This data point is huge because it tells us that while store count growth was the main contributor, it did not result in cannibalization, which means the underlying profile remains very strong.

There is a similar dynamic playing out in TOP TOY like in MNSO China, in that it is also leveraging the same interest consumption trend, only TOP TOY has higher average selling prices and targets a different customer profile. I think this makes sense since it does not result in cannibalization of the core MNSO brand.

And if TOP TOY continues to double every year, the timing of margin inflection should also come within the next few quarters. This, together with overseas market margin inflection and China SSS re-acceleration, should drive massive earnings growth.

Valuation

The upside remains very attractive to me. I have upgraded my growth assumptions to 25% CAGR through FY27 to reflect the data points in Q3 and also upgraded my net margin assumptions to 17% in FY27 to reflect the operating leverage. MNSO should generate ~RMB5.6 billion in net income by FY2027. If MNSO does perform as I expected, I believe the market will react very positively and, in the bull case, re-rate MNSO's multiple back to its historical average of 22x NTM PE. I don’t see why this can’t be the case since MNSO would be compounding earnings at >30%, which is much faster than what it did in early 2024 (the stock was 22x back then).

Risk

Aside from the risks I mentioned previously, a new risk I figured is the equity investment in Yonghui Superstores, which generated a RMB146 million loss this quarter. If performance continues to stay weak here, it could weigh on reported earnings and potentially divert management focus. Also, macro weakness in markets like Southeast Asia is still a drag on overseas growth, and if it gets worse, it could offset the growth from the US.

Conclusion

I reiterate my buy rating for MSNO. China SSS is accelerating, the US market is doing really well, and TOP TOY is becoming a real growth driver. All of these drive my upgrade for growth and margin assumptions through FY27. As accelerating revenue drives earnings growth, I see a path back to a higher multiple.

data.title: Miller Industries: Battling Short-Term Macro Pressures
data.description:
Miller Industries (MLR) has seen its stock decline over 40% since the beginning of 2025, positioning it today in an incrementally more attractive territory. Despite the large decline, though, I still don’t think it’s cheap enough for my liking to warrant a purchase. Miller has great long-term prospects and what I believe is a durable competitive position, but without higher growth and/or exceptional margin performance, outperformance from here appears unlikely.

Sales: Macro Pressures, Long-Term Recovery

Q3 sales came in at ~$179M, down a very material 43% from ~$314M posted in Q3 last year. Miller does sell accessories and parts alongside their core full-chassis installation sales; however, they don’t break out the sales between the two. The accessories/repair sales are inherently smaller as a percentage of sales by nature, but both sales types are likely down year-over-year given the consolidated trend, though accessory sales may be holding up much better.

From a pricing perspective, this was likely higher. They called out on the Q3 call how, “[e]arlier this year, we implemented a tariff surcharge on all new orders of manufactured product, along with additional price increases on accessories and parts.” What this additional price might have contributed to sales is unclear. From rough back-of-the-envelope math, tacking on tariffs on their non-labor COGS, which I estimate could be something like 70%-ish, something in the 2-3% range makes sense in terms of the approximate price contribution.

Broadly speaking, though, volumes were clearly negative irrespective of price in the results, for which a few items were attributed to this trend that we need to unpack. Part of the sales decline stemmed from a “...decline in chassis shipments, which were elevated in the prior year period as original equipment manufacturers (“OEMs”) recovered from supply chain disruptions.” In other words, they were up against an abnormally tougher comp from last year - and on a year-to-date basis too, with sales down ~40% on a nine-month/YTD basis.

Only partially consistent with this, however, they mentioned in their 10-K that the “back half” of FY24 was a little bit slower in terms of chassis deliveries than the first half - thanks to elevated shipments in the first half - suggesting that Q3’s comp is not as abnormal as the first half: “...while chassis supply from the manufacturers met our expectations in fiscal 2024, the timing of deliveries was weighted heavily in the first half of the year, resulting in a slowdown of demand in our distribution channels during the second half of the year.” Nonetheless, throughout FY24, chassis deliveries do appear higher than normal - as evidenced by their reported chassis inventory growing through 2024 - with that since coming down here in 2025.

So that’s one variable to the sales decline. However, there were also some inventory declines too, seemingly as a percentage of sales. For instance, they noted on the call that “...we are encouraged that inventory in our distribution channel continues to decrease, "and that they “...have preemptively adjusted production levels during the year to accelerate the reduction of field inventory.” Of course, if demand is lower, then they’ll technically “reduce” inventory too, but I furthermore interpret that as Miller - and their distributors - reducing inventory in excess of end-market demand/orders.

How much of the year-over-year sales decline was attributable to these two variables? We sort of have a rough approximation. If we adjust for shipment volume reductions attributable to the above, there’s still a clear decline in demand by their distributors, partly coming from a decline in end-market demand, consistent with their comments that the “...retail channel continues to delay purchases of new equipment due to macroeconomic uncertainty, which has left field inventory in our distribution channel elevated.” Indeed, as they noted last quarter, “Retail sales activity was down 20% quarter-over-quarter, resulting in a 30% decrease in order intake from our distributors.” Thus, with a 30% decline in orders, we can roughly confirm that the decline in sales (shipments) had mostly to do with reduced end-market demand and less to do with inventory right-sizing and a tough chassis comp.

This is largely an understandable order trend, although it’s not perfectly obvious. That is, from a use case angle, my understanding is that Miller’s products are primarily used in everyday vehicle towing, as per their investor deck and listed demand drivers, although yes, they also have a decent product portfolio that end customers use to handle larger vehicle and equipment towing - i.e., it’s not all just tied to vehicle accidents. But by and large, my inference is that end customers primarily use the equipment to handle broken-down or transported vehicles, which seems largely macro - e.g., we all still have to drive to work. Indeed, for what it’s worth, total miles traveled is reportedly higher year-over-year in the U.S.

Now, that said, I do understand that not all of their sales are ultimately tied to this variable - that is, miles driven - nor is this the only factor in an end customer's decision to purchase new equipment. For one, to the extent that less overall business activity is driving fewer trucks on the road (fewer miles), that could lead to fewer towings. Or from a different angle, to the extent that some of the equipment is used to transport equipment or vehicles specifically, that too could be of slightly lower volume today.

From an alternative angle, though, I find it reasonable that the general feeling of macro uncertainty has resulted in servicers deferring purchases. From a financing perspective, rates aren’t materially worse - if not better - than they were last year, so that’s probably not the core issue, although they are perhaps slightly higher than in 2023. That is, while I can’t find vehicle financing rate trends for their customers/vehicles specifically, broader new vehicle loan rate data indicates that financing is probably a little more attractive today than last year. However, recall that Miller - and likely their distributors - are pushing through price increases today, so that’s making it marginally worse. Furthermore, as is generally the case when macro conditions worsen, there tends to be a structural shift to used vehicles away from new vehicles, thus likely generating fewer orders versus 2024.

Management also called out a few other variables. They noted that “[i]nsurance premiums on their trucks have increased, interest rates for new trucks have risen, and the value of used trucks has fluctuated, affecting trading values and new equipment purchases.” And they also mentioned that “...what perhaps is the most uncertain is the Advanced Clean Truck regulation, which limits our ability to supply our products to customers in 6 large states.”

All of this is to say that while I don’t believe their use case-driven revenue has overwhelmingly declined for most of their end-users, I can understand why many have deferred purchases today versus last year. This has happened before too in prior economic downturns - going back to the 2007 to 2009 period, sales went from $400M to ~$238M, a ~40% decline. So, this behavior isn’t atypical.

From a competitive perspective, there’s always the outstanding question of whether they lost market share. Real-time data is hard to come by, particularly with their largest competitor, Jerr-Dan, being private. Qualitatively, though, there doesn't appear to be any quality degradation that would support share shift, or Jerr-Dan seeing a quality improvement to any material degree. Notably, Jerr-Dan’s management themselves posted an article citing weaker industry conditions, which is what I’d expect given Miller’s data here. So, while we don’t have their sales trend in quantitative form, we know they’re directionally negative too.

And if we zoom out, Miller’s history wouldn’t necessarily indicate share losses. Sales grew from $541M in 2015 to ~$818M in 2019, a nearly $280M increase in sales, all of which was organic. Then from 2019, they declined ~20% in 2010, but then - correspondingly with the macro - grew to ~$848M in 2022. They then acquired SHC in 2023, which partly boosted sales, but 2023 was then a strong year, with sales growing to ~$1.15B and then to ~$1.25B in 2024. We talked about the dynamics that led to a better-than-normalized 2024, but you can clearly see that the addition of ~$700M in sales over the past 9 years, with the bulk of that being organic. Thus, at least historically speaking, share losses are very unlikely, and without a great qualitative explanation that competitive dynamics have changed, it’s unlikely they’re losing share today too.

Notwithstanding these broader headwinds, they are seeing some nice growth in their public/government business. They noted on the call that they’re “...seeing greater demand in Europe as well as a notable increase in Request For Quote or RFQ activity for our military vehicles”, which they expect “...will continue into 2026 as we begin to prepare for production of military orders in 2027.” Supporting their belief for more demand over time, they’re moving “...forward with an EUR 8 million expansion at one of our facilities in France”.

Thinking about Miller going forward, for one, we know that today’s sales are below what a normalized level would be - i.e., if end-market demand stayed flat, the destocking would stop, and inventory would remain at a roughly similar level implying higher quarterly shipments by Miller to handle the go-forward orders. We know that orders were down about 30% year-over-year, so we could reasonably assume that the destocking was maybe 4-5-6% of the sales decline. If we assume it was, say, 5%, that would imply that a normalized level of sales would be about $190-195M.

There is some seasonality to their annual results, but we can essentially think about that number being a decent quarterly average, implying ~$770M in annual sales if inventory/order trends were stable. And this may be slightly understated a little bit too. They’re guiding for “$750 to $800 million for the 2025 fiscal year”, which implies Q4 sales of around $150-160M, which would imply a ~$60M sales decline from the ~$221M posted in Q4 last year. Now, FY24 results include the aforementioned inventory reduction headwind to sales, and Q4’s guidance specifically “will be impacted by the holidays and planned maintenance and downtime at our facilities”, so perhaps $780M is a fine, conservative starting point to the model.

From here, the question then becomes what long-term demand would look like. For one, they expect inventory to be in a more “normalized” position in FY26, but we’re sort of already including this in the $780M figure, so I’m not adding anything to that. From a macro standpoint, it’s clear that today’s order levels are not normalized to the environment. That is, today’s demand is impacted by macro uncertainty more so than it is from actual end-market demand - i.e., miles traveled, vehicles towed, etc. This, then, should revert long-term back to a higher level consistent with my expectations that the macro will do the same.

To this end, I don’t really have great quantitative insight into order volumes by year. However, to reference the aforementioned data point, we do know that orders are 30% lower than in 2024. Can they get back to that order volume? Probably not on a normalized basis. We referenced some of the repo volume trends, which likely drove some orders, but then also referenced the abnormal increase in chassis supply, which likely allowed the distributors to accept more orders than perhaps a typical year. And that’s particularly true if there’s been some pent up demand from prior years when chassis supply was more limited. An unscientific guess, then, might suggest that maybe two-thirds of that order decline was actually market driven with the residual being an incomparable comp.

If true, that would imply that if we took today’s run rate and translated it into that hypothetical normalized order volume level, Miller would be doing about $185M more in sales annually, or ~$965M. This wouldn’t be all that abnormal either relative to the pre-COVID performance. That is, we know they did ~$818M in sales in 2019 - if we make some pricing adjustments for the inflation post-2020 alongside some modest organic growth and SHC, that $818M could reasonably translate into a mid-$900M figure.

Beyond this, there appear to be some organic growth opportunities over time with their military/government business, like I noted earlier, which they expect. I don’t anticipate share gains given their already very large position and am okay assuming they’ll maintain share over time, but I feel comfortable assuming they can grow their international business, but retail and government.

Added all up, between price, normalization of inventory and order levels, and then some modest organic growth success, this business could be posting ~$1.1B in sales by FY30, and something closer to $1.3B by FY35. Of course, this is largely contingent on orders growing to levels hypothesized earlier, but I feel decently confident in this assumption given historical levels plus general macro independence of their end-markets.

Margins: Operating Leverage Over Time

Q3 gross margins came in at 14.2%, up 80 bps from 13.4% last year despite the material decrease in sales. EBIT margins, however, declined from 6.3% last year to 2.3% this year, or ~400 bps lower.

Thinking about the growth in gross margins, part of this was likely benefited from price, as I’m inferring that the prices they’re pushing through today better match the tariff/inflation they’ve seen. That is, it’s reasonable to me that in 2024, they were incurring inflation without a corresponding recognition of higher price, but that has seemingly flipped with the tariff situation stabilizing slightly with the price now catching up. And assisting this has been their shift to purchase “key materials from low-tariff geographies to maintain our margins and keep our cost for raw materials as low as possible.”

Moreover, though, they attribute most of the improvement to mix, citing a “higher percentage of unit deliveries compared to chassis shipments,” a trend I’ve referenced earlier with chassis shipments down. Notably, too, these variables are both more than offsetting fixed COGS deleverage. That is, COGS will be comprised of freight and materials that are directly tied to their units, but there’s also labor and overhead that’s going to be more fixed, so as sales have declined versus last year, there’s going to be less sales leverage per dollar within those buckets.

Now, as a semi-segue into SG&A, it’s worth mentioning that they “took decisive action in the third quarter” and decided on “right-sizing our costs for the current environment.” Said differently, they reduced “headcount by approximately 150 positions across 3 of our U.S. manufacturing facilities.” So, this effectively offsets some of the fixed COGS deleverage too, but given the extent of the sales decline, it’s unreasonable to believe that they can match 2024’s leverage levels notwithstanding these declines.

On a similar note, SG&A de-levered materially - while sales declined nearly $140M year-over-year, SG&A declined by ~$1M to ~$21M. Part of this reflects a one-time headwind of $900K resulting from “costs related to an enhanced retirement program the Company offered to all U.S. employees aged 65 and above during the third quarter of 2025”. But clearly, that doesn’t materially change the deleverage picture, notwithstanding that they also saw some tailwinds from “cost savings initiatives and lower executive compensation expenses.”

All of this is understandable too. Their personnel that handle their distributor relationships can’t really be downsized just because inventory levels and orders are lower - if there’s a distributor, they need to support them. This extends to their back-office staff as well, which needs to be maintained notwithstanding sales volumes. It’s possible that their marketing spend declined, but to the extent that most of their marketing spend is trade-show oriented, it’s hard to imagine there were material savings here either.

Bridging all of this into a go-forward model, from a mix perspective, they expect chassis inventory to increase to more closely match body inventory, which will likely imply about a 50-ish bps headwind per the year-over-year results, or ~13.6% margins as they capture more chassis revenue, so don’t be alarmed if margins decline in the near-term. From a price/cost standpoint, my understanding is that at least on today’s cost profile, price is where they want it to be - they’re not anticipating further increases. Furthermore, though, I expect them to be able to maintain this relationship over time given the competitive dynamics. And with the cost environment somewhat more stabilized today, I’m not anticipating any near-term dislocation.

From a leverage standpoint though, this should be a (very) material tailwind over time per my model. Cost-wise, they’re doing some right-sizing today to adjust to lower demand, but beyond this, I don’t see any latent potential to structurally reduce costs further to achieve long-term savings. Instead, as sales assumptively grow to $1.3B over time, there’s no reason they can’t get back to operating margin levels in the 6-7% range just as they’ve captured in prior periods (pre-COVID and post-COVID) when their cost profile was more normalized relative to higher demand. What would make their history incomparable would be a materially different mix or cost profile, but I don’t infer either of those to be so structurally different that such margin is unachievable. After all, they just posted nearly 7% EBIT margins in FY24.

So, assuming the above to be true, if they are to grow to $1.3B in sales and earn a more mature, normalized margins of say, 6.5%, which bakes in the benefit of operating leverage without any structural headwinds from mix or price/cost relative to today, that would imply EBIT of ~$85M.

Valuation

From a capital spend perspective, capex can be a little lumpy depending on the timing of projects/facility maintenance and improvements - e.g., expansion of France’s capacity. For instance, they spent ~$29M on capex in 2022, but then ~$12M in 2023. If we average it out, however, capex tends to run in the 1.75% range. Year-to-date though, they’ve only spent about $8.6M, or ~1.4% of sales. But given my expectations for growth over time, I don’t mind conservatively modeling this at 1.75%.

As for the rest of their free-cash-flow, I noted earlier their decision to acquire SHC, for which they paid ~$17.4M. Beyond that, however, while they’ll throw some share repurchases in there, they’re primarily just shipping cash back to shareholders via dividends. Over the past few years, they have raised some debt to fund their capital expenditures and (presumably) dividend, but this has largely been because of the mismatch of working capital. That is, as they and their distributors were pushing to increase inventory to more normalized levels, this was deferring receivables collection. However, the opposite is now happening today with receivables down from ~$313M at the start of the year to ~$234M today - a ~$80M decline - as inventory is being reduced. Net, I’m okay assuming 100% dividends going forward as the opportunity cost - share repurchases - should generate a similar IRR and thus have no material impact on the model.

Putting the model together, I’m assuming FY35 sales and EBIT of $1.3B and $85M, respectively. Backing out taxes at 27% (a blend of U.S. and foreign statutory tax rates), D&A at 1.75%, and capex at 1.75%, I get to a FY35 free-cash-flow figure of ~$61M per annum.

At today’s price of nearly $39/share, this doesn’t translate into any huge outperformance. If I add in about $300M in cash generated in the interim, these economics imply about a 12% assuming a low-single-digit growth rate beyond FY35, which I think is more than reasonable given their competitive position and large geographical coverage at that point. If they are to grow more materially internationally than I’m modeling, that would be a slight tailwind. However, I feel as if I’m already giving them decent credit here and on the long-term margin front, so I don’t think there’s a huge variance around the implied return here.

Conclusion

All considered, I like Miller as a business - I think they have a durable long-term demand stream (though there is some long-term risk of driverless cars) and solid competitive position that I’d want to own. However, at today’s price point against my long term assumptions and near-term demand risk, I’m not interested in buying just yet.

data.title: Lenz Therapeutics: A Close Look
data.description:
Introduction and How I Found LENZ

Encouraged by my recent successful pick of Nuvation Bio, I went back to the FDA’s latest list of approved novel drugs, looking for similar small-cap biotech opportunities. My criteria are simple but tough: I need to find biotech opportunities that I, being a lawyer, can intelligently analyze with my fourth-grade math and critical thinking. Specifically, I look for biotech stocks whose major regulatory risks are behind them because of an FDA approval, or a very probable FDA approval. Second, their commercial prospects must be capable of being evaluated with rough numbers rather than speculation. Third, the stock must be underpriced relative to such commercial prospects.

Lenz Therapeutics, Inc. (LENZ) stood out because VIZZ, its newly approved presbyopia drop, sits in a category with only one real commercial predecessor—AbbVie’s Vuity. That combination of FDA approval and a small market cap of below $1 billion makes LENZ a potential candidate more than the typical pre-revenue biotech with lofty valuation. With about $200 million in cash, LENZ has the adequate runway to launch VIZZ. With no meaningful drug candidate pipeline, LENZ’s valuation is essentially the market’s estimate of VIZZ’s future revenue.

An Overview of Presbyopia Market

Presbyopia affects nearly every adult over forty-five, including yours truly. Most professional analysts have rather optimistic outlooks for the total addressable market and/or VIZZ’s market share within it. For example, S&P Global confidently projects that VIZZ will reach about $1 billion in annual peak sales (see below).

If you put your faith blindly in this estimate—which may or may not materialize—you can stop reading my article now and be confident that LENZ’s stock price will at least triple as VIZZ’s revenue skyrockets. Why? The rule of thumb is that a biotech company may command a price-to-peak-sales multiple of 3–5, thereby giving LENZ a fair market cap of $3–$5 billion.

Let’s look at another chart (see blow), which projects that the seven leading presbyopia markets would achieve $2.5 billion of annual revenue by 2035. If we reconcile these two projections, S&P Global’s $1 billion peak-sales forecast essentially requires VIZZ to take up about one-third of the TAM.

Is that plausible, realistic, likely, probable, or merely speculation?

A Closer Look at Vuity, VIZZ’s Closest Competitor

It is always better not only to look at the big picture from 3,000 feet above but also to zoom in on the more granular details. One need look no further than the same S&P Global chart above.

There you can see Vuity, an eye drop developed by Allergan before the company was acquired by AbbVie for the same indication, presbyopia. Apparently, S&P Global was very optimistic about the future of VIZZ and very pessimistic about Vuity.

Let's check out Vuity’s commercial performance so far. After launching in late 2021, Vuity reached roughly 120,000 U.S. users in the full year 2022 according to this professional journal article, out of about 100 million Americans with presbyopia. I spent an entire weekend Googling but I could not find Vuity’s actual annual revenue figures, probably because AbbVie never disclosed it.

The best that I could find is the estimate produced by the same S&P Global article. There the author stated, "Vuity, typically administered once or twice a day, is forecast to generate $117 million in 2026." Note that 2026 would be the sixth full year after Vuity's commercial launch.

Also note that in September, 2022 AbbVie took a $770 million impairment related to Vuity and scaled down the drug’s marketing effort, confirming its disappointing commercial performance

Will VIZZ Perform Better?

It is still possible that VIZZ may perform better than Vuity. So let’s place them both under the microscope before we zoom out to see the big picture again.

VIZZ approaches the same market with a cleaner pharmacologic profile. Aceclidine, the active agent in VIZZ, selectively targets the sphincter muscle without stimulating the ciliary muscle, reducing (but not eliminating) headaches, distance blur, and night-vision issues. Clinical data suggest up to 10 hours of near-vision improvement, longer than what many Vuity users reported. In addition, it is reported that Vuity may have contributed to rare but real cases of retina detachment.

So VIZZ’s improved safety, tolerability and duration may somewhat address the weaknesses that limited Vuity’s revenue potential. However, whether and to what extent this translates into better real-world adherence is hard to tell. Hopefully this will become clear quickly, as refill rates in the first year will inform—if not determine—VIZZ’s commercial trajectory.

Even if VIZZ performs better in the market, we should not lose sight of the big picture, which is: most people use inexpensive reading glasses to manage presbyopia. Consumers have no medical need to seek pharmacologic treatment. The real addressable market probably consists only of those willing to pay out of pocket for the convenience of not carrying reading glasses and those who prefer their appearance without spectacles.

When you think about it, administering eye drops once or twice a day can complicate a daily routine more than simply finding a pair of glasses. And if the eye drop has any side effect, being it headache, red eye or irritation, that tilts the balance back again toward wearing eyeglasses.

More importantly, we are all, at least in the short term and for purchase decisions of simple things, rational economic beings. That is to say, if the eye drop costs us more than a pair of reading glasses, the company selling us the eye drop needs really justify such additional cost with very compelling benefits of such eyedrop. Vuity costs about $800-$1000 a year, and VIZZ is expected to come with a similar price tag. A simple Googling indicates that most people pay less than $200 for a pair of reading glasses.

In short, I feel that the marginal improvement of VIZZ over Vuity is unlikely to change most patients/consumers' decisions here.

Quantitative Revenue Model

VIZZ’s commercial value can be modeled by users multiplied by annual refills multiplied by net realized price. Vuity’s net price was likely around $70 per bottle; using $65 for VIZZ is reasonable.

In a conservative scenario, VIZZ barely outperforms Vuity. Perhaps 300,000 people use it and refill twice a year, producing about $40 million annually—far too low to justify LENZ’s roughly $750 million enterprise value.

A bullish base case envisions around 1,000,000 U.S. users—still less than 1% of presbyopes—refilling 4 times per year. That yields about $260 million in annual revenue, consistent with the valuation LENZ commands today. The market seems to be pricing in this outcome. Note this requires VIZZ to perform substantially better than Vuity.

A more bullish scenario assumes VIZZ becomes the preferred presbyopia drop globally. Perhaps 2,500,000 consistent users worldwide refill 6 times per year, generating $500–$650 million annually. This upside resembles S&P Global's published Visible Alpha’s consensus and could support a stock price meaningfully above today’s level.

The challenge here is that I do not have any more intelligent insight than the average market participant as to which scenario will prove to be more aligned with market reality in the next 6, 12, or 24 months. Here my fourth-grade math can only take me so far. I simply do not have greater visibility into the future market performance of VIZZ.

One Bright Spot: The Insiders Are Buying

My followers may remember that the stock of Nuvation Bio piqued my interest during early summer 2025 because almost all of its C-suite executives were buying shares. Insider purchases are by no means "dispositive", as we lawyers would say. However, Peter Lynch always notes that insiders may sell for many reasons, but they buy only when they are bullish.

Here are the details of recent insider purchases; you may assign whatever weight you deem appropriate to those data points.

Date

Name

Title

Transaction
